summary: Teaches that past participles (-ado/-ido) can be used as adjectives with estar (está cerrado, está perdida). The participle is invariable after haber but agrees in gender/number as an adjective. Explains ser vs estar with these adjectives, including the 'es una persona + adjective' pattern.

teachings.summary: The -ado/-ido form used after haber can also be used as an adjective. After haber it never changes, but as an adjective it agrees in gender and number. These adjectives almost always go with estar, not ser.
teachings.rule: Participle after haber: invariable (lo he cerrado, la he cerrado). As adjective: agrees in gender/number (cerrado/cerrada/cerrados/cerradas). Almost always uses estar.

examples.english: I have closed it (invariable after haber)
I have closed it (feminine, still cerrado after haber)
It is closed (the door - feminine adjective)

teachings.notes: After haber: always invariable. As adjective: agrees with noun. Almost always with estar, with a couple of exceptions.

teachings.conceptName: Ser vs estar with participle adjectives
teachings.summary: Participle adjectives normally go with estar. But when saying 'it is a [noun]' with ser, the adjective is secondary to the noun classification. 'La silla está mojada' (the chair is wet) vs 'Es una silla mojada' (it is a wet chair).
teachings.rule: Use estar when the adjective is the main point (la silla está mojada). Use ser when classifying with a noun (es una silla mojada). Es una persona cerrada is fine because ser goes with the noun persona.

examples.english: He is closed to the idea (estar)
He/she is a closed person (ser + noun)
The chair is wet (estar for state)
It is a wet chair (ser for classification)

teachings.notes: When you say 'es un/una [noun]', ser pairs with the noun, not the adjective. The adjective is secondary.

teachings.summary: Cansado (tired) is actually the past participle of cansar (to tire). Me canso = I get tired. Me he cansado = I've gotten tired/fed up.

examples.english: I get tired / I get fed up
I've gotten tired / I've had enough
